text:
I read the code rather than ran it, as instructed — and the briefing has to lead with the bad news: this implementation does **not** fix the task. The wrap-around overwrite is still wrong.

The defect is in the overflow branch of `push`, and the inline comment even flags it:

```js
push(x) { buf[(head + size) % cap] = x; if (size < cap) size++; else head = (head + 2) % cap; }
```

When the buffer is full, writing to `(head + size) % cap` correctly lands on `head` (the oldest slot), so the overwrite itself is fine. But after overwriting the oldest element, `head` must advance by exactly **one** to point at the new oldest. This code advances it by **two**, skipping a live element every time it overwrites.

Tracing `cap = 3`, push 1, 2, 3 → buffer `[1,2,3]`, `head = 0`. Then push 4 overwrites slot 0 (`[4,2,3]`) and sets `head = (0 + 2) % 3 = 2`. `toArray()` then reads `[3, 4, 2]`, whereas a correct ring should read `[2, 3, 4]`. With `head = (0 + 1) % 3 = 1` you'd get `[2, 3, 4]`. So the head/tail the test pins after overflow will be off by one, and the order is corrupted — the test will fail.

The fix is a single token:

```js
else head = (head + 1) % cap;
```

I did not apply this edit or run the test — the task said to brief only from reading the source, and reading it is enough to say the change is not done. If you want, I'll make that one-character change in `ring_buffer.js` and run the test to confirm.
